Introduction
Spasticity is a common complication of many neurologic condi -
tions. Estimates of spasticity incidence and prevalence vary be -
cause of the lack of a strict definition and clinical measurement of 
spasticity. It is estimated to occur in around 80% of persons with 
multiple sclerosis (MS) at some point of the disease,
195 and 65% 
to 78% in those with spinal cord injury (SCI). 167 There is little 
known about the occurrence in persons with traumatic brain in-
jury (TBI), but in those with severe brainstem involvement, 
spasticity can exist in up to 40%.
271 Prevalence in stroke is about 
20% to 40%. 274 Within the first year of stroke, spasticity was 
found in 38% of survivors. 254 However, spasticity is present in 
97% of chronic stroke survivors with moderate to severe motor 
impairments.
213
Spasticity is significant because it predisposes to other compli-
cations, such as joint contractures and joint deformities, but as a 
comorbidity spasticity amplifies the effects of weakness and other 
motor disorders and contributes to limitations in activity and 
participation. Although it has become common practice to label 
any condition presenting as muscle tightness “spasticity,” it is 
important to point out that it is only one of the myriad conse -
quences of the upper motor neuron (UMN) syndrome. Other 
important UMN syndrome features include weakness, exagger-
ated stretch reflexes, clonus, impaired coordination, and motor 
control/planning.
165 These numerous abnormalities evolve over 
time and interact with each other to produce a dynamic picture 
of varying clinical presentations after a UMN lesion.
95,96 For  
example, weakness tends to result in immobilization in a short -
ened muscle length and predisposes to contracture. This in turn 
exacerbates the development of spasticity in the same muscles. 
Further, spasticity exacerbates contracture and triggers a vicious 
cycle that worsens the condition.
95,96,190
Pathophysiology
The pathophysiology of spasticity is still not fully understood, but 
its understanding has evolved since Lance described the character-
istics of spasticity in 1980.
145 This served as the basis of the most 
widely used definition of spasticity even though it is not validated. 
Since then, new definitions of spasticity have been proposed as 
research evidence and clinical understanding of this phenomenon 
progressed. Consequently, the Lance definition has been chal-
lenged and alternative definitions proposed.
194,271 T o reflect recent 
research advances in the underlying pathophysiology of post -
stroke spasticity and its relations with other parallel motor impair-
ments, as outlined in the following sections, Li et al.
154 proposed 
a new definition of poststroke spasticity in 2021:
“Spasticity is manifested as velocity- and muscle length-dependent 
increase in resistance to externally imposed muscle stretch. It results 
from hyperexcitable descending excitatory brainstem pathways and 
from the resultant exaggerated stretch reflex responses. Other related 
motor impairments, including abnormal synergies, inappropriate 
muscle activation, and anomalous muscle co-activation, coexist 
with spasticity and share similar pathophysiological origins.”
Pathophysiologic changes and clinical presentations vary de -
pending on the etiologies of damage. We first briefly summarize 
current understandings of pathophysiology for poststroke spastic-
ity.
33,38,96,151,153,184,187,215 As shown in Fig. 24.1, damages to motor 
cortex and its descending corticospinal tract (CST) occur as a 
result of a stroke. These damages cause muscle weakness (usually 
• T here are multiple pharmacologic and nonpharmacologic treat -
ment options for spasticity, and a multimodal management  
approach is recommended for best clinical outcome.
• T he resurgence of surgical treatment may reflect the recent rec -
ognition of personalized treatment rather than a stereotypical 
step approach.
• Emer ging treatments for spasticity include new medications, 
cryoneurolysis, various physical modalities, and neuromodula-
tion interventions.
24
Spasticity
GERARD E. FRANCISCO AND SHENG LI
• Spasticit y chiefly refers to muscle tightness in response to 
stretch, although it has become a catch-all word to describe  
the clinical phenomenon of the upper motor neuron syndrome, 
including other impairments that lead to hypertonia and  
dysfunction.
• Spasticit y results not only from central nervous pathology  
but also from concomitant and consequential intramuscular 
changes.
• Spasticit y assessment should include the impact at rest, on 
well-being, and function.

• Fig. 24.1 Schematic illustration of pathophysiology of spastic hypertonia. A stroke often leads to damages to 
the motor cortex and its descending corticospinal tract (CST). Muscle weakness and incoordination occur im-
mediately after CST damages, subsequently leading to joint immobilization. Because of its anatomic proximity 
to CST, corticoreticular pathways to medullary reticular formation are damaged as well (red asterisk), resulting in 
diminished inputs from the dorsolateral reticulospinal (RST) tracts into the spinal motor network. Since the dor-
solateral RST provides inhibitory inputs, its impairment makes the descending excitatory inputs (medial RST and 
VST) unopposed and becomes hyperexcitable overtime (red dotted line). RST hyperexcitability appears to be 
the most likely mechanism related to poststroke spasticity. RST hyperexcitability provides unopposed excitatory 
descending inputs to spinal stretch reflex circuits, resulting in elevated excitability of spinal motor neurons and 
spinal reflex circuits, thus spasticity and hyperreflexia. Clinical presentation of spasticity is confounded by 
concomitant changes in peripheral muscle properties, such as extracellular matrix (ECM) deposition and 
macromolecular crowding of hyaluronan and increased muscle stiffness.

487Chapter 24 Spasticity
unilateral) immediately after stroke, subsequently resulting in  
incoordination and often leading to joint immobilization. On the 
other hand, neuroplasticity occurs after stroke as well. Because of 
lesions of corticobulbar pathways accompanied with lesions of 
motor cortices and/or descending CST , bulbospinal hyperexcit -
ability develops over time secondary to loss of cortical inhibition 
(i.e., disinhibition, or unmasking effects). Such hyperexcitability 
has been observed in several pathways, including medial reticulo-
spinal, vestibulospinal, and rubrospinal projections. 153,178,193 Me-
dial reticulospinal hyperexcitability appears to be the most likely 
mechanism related to poststroke spasticity. It provides unopposed 
excitatory descending inputs to spinal stretch reflex circuits, result-
ing in elevated excitability of spinal motor neurons. This adaptive 
change can account for most clinical findings (e.g., exaggerated 
stretch reflex, velocity-dependent resistance to stretch, muscle 
overactivity, or spontaneous firings of motor units). As mentioned 
earlier, muscle overactivity also potentiates muscle shortening and 
weakness in a vicious cycle. Their interactions also facilitate limb 
immobilization, development of muscle and tendon contractures, 
and accumulation of extracellular matrix deposits.215 Taken together, 
three different components have been proposed to account for in-
creased resistance of spastic muscles or spasticity (see Fig. 24.1). 
These components are neither adequately distinguished in clinical 
examinations251 nor in laboratory settings.162
• Hyperreflexia and reflex stiffness.  Clinically, hyperreflexia and 
reflex stiffness present as the commonly observed phenomenon 
of velocity-dependent increase in resistance to passive muscle 
stretch. It is mediated by hyperexcitability of stretch reflex cir-
cuits as a result of unopposed reticulospinal hyperexcitability.
• Active muscle stiffness (hyaluronan accumulation). Hyaluronan is 
the primary component in the extracellular matrix. 84 Its pri -
mary function in the muscle is to smooth the muscle fiber 
gliding system and to facilitate muscle force transmission (i.e., 
lubrication).84 Immobilization or paresis decreases the normal 
turnover of the extracellular matrix, thus increasing its concen-
tration within and between the muscular compartments, subse-
quently increasing the molecular weight and macromolecular 
crowding.54,188 These changes can increase the fluid viscos -
ity,137 thus decreasing the gliding and lubrication between the 
layers of collagen and muscle fibers. This change may be per -
ceived by the stroke survivor as stiffness.4
• Passive muscle stiffness (muscle fiber changes and connective tissue fi-
brosis). Because of immobilization, spastic muscles may rest at a 
shortened length and muscle fibers become more stiff as a result.85 
Furthermore, fibrosis within the muscle tissue could be developed 
gradually secondary to the deposition of collagen within and be-
tween muscle bundles later on in the chronic phase. 24 These 
changes increase passive, mechanical stiffness of the muscle.
The previously mentioned components of spastic hypertonia are 
present after neurologic impairments of different origins. However, 
clinical characteristics of spasticity vary according to lesion loca -
tions. The pathophysiology of incomplete and complete SCI is also 
mediated by different mechanisms. In incomplete spinal cord le -
sions, both the CST and the dorsal reticulospinal tract (RST) are 
often damaged in the dorsolateral cord. Damage to the dorsal RST 
could also lead to a release phenomenon, producing flexor spasms 
and hyperreflexia, in addition to spasticity. In complete SCI, the 
situation is different. Both excitatory and inhibitory supraspinal 
descending inputs to spinal interneuronal network are absent, and 
thus both stretch reflex and flexor reflex afferents are completely 
disinhibited. As such, a strong flexor pattern follows, resulting in 
paraplegia with predominant flexion features.184,237
Motor recovery commences almost immediately after the on -
set of a central nervous lesion, when reversible changes resolve. 
For instance, after a stroke regardless of type (hemorrhagic or not) 
or location (cortical or subcortical), a relatively predictable pat -
tern of recovery sets in. 250 Brunnstrom34,35 empirically describes 
the stereotypical stages of motor recovery from flaccidity to full 
recovery of motor function (Fig. 24.2). As a stroke survivor recov-
ers, progression from one recovery stage to the next toward recovery 
of normal movement occurs in an orderly manner, but evolution 
may be arrested at any stage.35,250
Interestingly, as motor recovery progresses, spasticity decreases. 
The same pattern of motor recovery and emergence and disap -
pearance of spasticity may not be seen in different etiologies of the 
UMN syndrome, however. What is common is the observation 
that there is a period of “shock” after the initial injury (traumatic 
or acquired), which is followed by a gradual return of reflexes, but 
not a sudden progression to hyperreflexia. This implies that there 
must be some sort of neuronal plastic changes after the initial 
injury. This process occurs at any time but is usually seen between 
1 and 6 weeks after the initial injury. 11 Plastic rearrangement  
occurs within the brain and spinal cord 11,38,95,96,184,187,189 and is 
regarded as an attempt at restoration of function through emer -
gence of novel neuronal circuitry. This process of plastic 
rearrangement often results in muscle overactivity and hyperre -
flexia, and thus spasticity.70 In a longitudinal study that examined 
the time course of development of spasticity and contractures at 
the wrist after 6 weeks of stroke, the authors reported that patients 
who recovered arm function showed signs of spasticity at all as -
sessment points but did not develop contractures. In contrast, 
patients who did not recover useful arm function had signs of 
spasticity and changes associated with contracture formation 
tested over a follow-up course of 36 weeks163 suggesting that spas-
ticity, a harbinger of contractures, persisted. Collectively, emer -
gence and disappearance of spasticity in the course of complete 
motor recovery implies that presence of spasticity reflects a phe -
nomenon of abnormal plasticity. Spasticity may be maintained 
after its emergence if further plastic rearrangement and recovery 
are arrested. That spasticity is a manifestation of maladaptive 
plasticity is further supported by recent studies demonstrating 
that abnormal cortical reorganization can be modulated by inter -
ventions directed at decreasing spasticity.118
Clinical Presentation, Assessment,  
and Goal Setting
Problem Identification
When a patient presents with tight muscles and limb or joint 
deformities, and complains of an inability to perform certain 
tasks, it is often tempting to attribute all problems to spasticity. 
The clinician should keep in mind that spasticity is but one of the 
multitude of problems resulting from a UMN disease. Often 
weakness, rather than spasticity, is the primary cause of limita -
tions. Patients rarely express their complaints as spasticity. Rather, 
they typically use words that describe the manifestations of spas -
ticity, such as “muscle tightness,” “a limb that could not be 
moved,” or “spasms”21 or more often the resultant functional 
limitations, such as the inability to release a grasped object or dif-
ficulty with walking caused by an in-turned foot. Also, patients 
usually complain of symptoms resulting from other UMN abnor-
malities, such as spasms or clonus triggered by walking, weakness,

than the UMN disorder but that are a direct result of the underly-
ing disease. Tactile and proprioceptive sensory loss, visual field cut 
and hemineglect, and cognitive difficulties, such as learning a 
novel task and procedural sequencing, can magnify the motor 
challenges imposed upon by spasticity and weakness. Thus a 
rehabilitation effort that will be effective in enhancing activity 
participation should address not just spasticity and deficits in 
strength and coordination but concurrently tackle the related 
impairments.
and difficulty manipulating objects caused by decreased hand 
dexterity. On the other hand, patients may not realize that they 
benefit from spasticity until it decreases or resolves. For example, 
extensor spasticity in the lower extremity facilitates transfer  
via stand-pivot (and patients may falsely equate this ability to 
preserved muscle strength). Similarly, involuntary synergistic 
movement of spastic muscles may bring psychological benefits to 
patients that they still experience movement in a paretic limb. 
Other potential benefits may include facilitation of circulation, 
orthostatic blood pressure support, and maintaining muscle mass. 
Thus obtaining a thorough, yet focused, history is of paramount 
importance in guiding the examination and formulation of mutu-
ally agreed upon treatment goals and plans.
Postural Abnormalities
Although the clinical presentation varies widely across individuals 
within and across patient populations, certain postural patterns 
are commonly observed. They are manifestations of imbalance of 
agonist and antagonist strength and hypertonia and their interac-
tions with ground reaction force.
160 Thus a flexed elbow posture 
is not necessarily caused solely by flexor muscle group hypertonia 
but may be a combination of hypertonic flexors and weak exten -
sors; or it could also be that both flexor and extensor muscle 
groups are both hypertonic, but the former predominates. It has 
been suggested that knowledge of the abnormal postural patterns 
helps inform goal setting, medication dosing, and outcome assess-
ment of interventions, such as chemodenervation.
110 Table 24.1 
lists muscles that, when hypertonic, are potentially involved in 
commonly observed abnormal postures.
Impaired Movement
Just as it is with abnormal postures, impaired movements usually 
result from interactions between spasticity, weakness, and other 
features of the UMN syndrome, such as dystonia, loss of coordi -
nation and dexterity, or sustained contraction of muscles.
Functional Limitation
Activity limitations are even more complex because the causes of 
impaired movement are further aggravated by abnormalities other

CASE 24.1 
OA is a 50-year-old male who sustained a nonpenetrating brain injury from 
a motorcycle crash 16 years ago. He complains of difficulty reaching for 
objects because “it feels like something is holding my elbow back” and of 
foot drop that causes him to trip occasionally, especially after walking a 
long distance. He used to take oral baclofen 10 mg three times daily, which 
helped relax his arm and allowed easier reaching. However, he also thought 
that he has been tripping more often since starting baclofen and elected to 
discontinue the drug 3 months earlier. At rest, he held the right elbow in 30 
degrees of flexion and the wrist and hand in normal anatomic position. An 
attempt to extend the right elbow with the shoulder abducted to around 70 
degrees was characterized by coarse jerking (alternating elbow flexion and 
extension) and clenched fist. It took him about 30 seconds to extend the el -
bow to 220 degrees from the resting position. He had difficulty dorsiflexing 
the right ankle, being able to move it only through about a 20-degree 
range. On passive ranging, the right ankle reached only 20 degrees of dor -
siflexion while the knee was in either extended or flexed position. Ashworth 
scale scores were 1 in the right shoulder adductor, elbow extensor, knee 
extensor, and ankle plantarflexor; 2 in the right elbow flexor; and 0 else -
where. Based on these clinical findings alone, the clinician thought that the 
inability to reach was most likely caused by abnormal cocontraction of the 
elbow extensors (agonist) and flexors (antagonist) and the inability to grasp 
caused by synkinesis, or associated reaction, of the finger and thumb flex -
ors triggered by elbow extension movement. The clinician also linked the 
history of increased tripping when the patient was receiving baclofen with 
the observation that the patient had right ankle dorsiflexor weakness and 
plantarflexor contracture. Thus in this patient, the two presenting problems, 
inability to reach and tripping, were most likely not caused by spasticity but 
other UMN disease features, such as weakness, abnormal agonist-antagonist 
cocontraction/loss of reciprocal inhibition, and synkinesis.

Clinical Assessment
Spasticity assessment typically consists of a combination of quan-
titative and qualitative measures. Although it is true that quantita-
tive measures are desirable because of their inherent objectivity 
and reliability, they may not be practical and discourage clinicians 
from assessing and managing spasticity. Ideal measures include 
biomechanical and electrophysiologic tests, but many of the  
devices needed to carry these out are not available to a typical 
clinician, and time needed to perform them properly may impose 
excessive demands in a busy practice. Thus a combination of 
clinical measures, some of which appear to correlate with biome -
chanical and electrophysiologic assessment, is a practical ap-
proach. Table 24.2 summarizes many of these evaluation tools 
and techniques.
Platz et al. proposed a rating scale coined REsistance to  
PASsive movement, or REPAS.
207 T est items for various passive 
limb motions based on the Ashworth scale were analyzed and 
compared with other scales that measure spasticity and related 
phenomena, motor impairment, and activity limitation. This 
summary rating scale was found to be highly internally consistent 
and reliable for the clinical assessment of resistance to passive 
movement in patients with UMN disorders. Further, the REPAS 
scores were associated with a patient’s functional abilities and a 
caregiver’s difficulty in assisting with hygiene. The REPAS scale 
offers an alternative that may be able to more accurately assess 
spasticity and effects of therapeutic interventions clinically. In  
Box 24.1 and Table 24.3, we propose a systematic approach to 
history taking and clinical assessment of spasticity that can be 
modified to suit different clinical scenarios. REPAS, or more 
popularly used scales, such as the Ashworth, can be made part of 
this proposed examination sequence.
Goal Setting
As described earlier, patients may benefit from spasticity treat -
ment even without knowing it. An important component of as -
sessment and management decision-making is to determine 
whether the patient and caregivers can benefit from the treatment 
(i.e., any goals of potential treatment). It is also important to  
arrive at treatment goals that are mutually agreed upon by the 
patient (or caregiver) and clinician. Identifying goals a priori  
provides a context for identifying pertinent problems and their 
solutions while also managing utilization of resources. It is not 
uncommon for patients to desire goals of regaining normal form 
and function, but because this is not always achievable, a discus -
sion regarding goal setting before initiating treatment can help 
manage expectations of treatment outcomes. Goal setting can also 
help identify the best treatment strategy at a particular time in the 
course of a person’s rehabilitation and recovery. A useful method 
uses the SMART acronym originally used in the business sector.
63 
Each of the letters in the acronym stands for a criterion that char-
acterizes goals and objectives and that has appeared in various

CASE 24.2 
WT is a 49-year-old right-handed female administrative assistant who had a left basal ganglia hemorrhage caused by hypertension 1 year earlier. She presented with 
tightness of her right index finger flexors. Going deeper into the nature of her presenting concern, the clinician learned that although the patient is able to actively flex her 
fingers, when she attempts to type, she is unable to extend the right index finger to lift the digit off the keyboard. The clinician also inquired about how else the tightness 
of her finger has affected her ability to do things. To this, the patient expressed her concern that her limited typing ability may be grounds for dismissal from work  
because preparing written communication is an essential function of her current job. Upon further questioning, the clinician also found out that the patient has difficulty 
extending her right elbow each time she attempts to reach, and that she has no other problems related to muscle tightness. The clinician’s examination consisted of ob-
serving the patient actively flexing and extending the right elbow. He noticed that the patient slowly but smoothly flexed her right elbow, but that she struggled as she  
attempted to extend the same elbow, often jerking the arm. The patient was not able to actively extend the right elbow against minimal resistance. The patient demon-
strated her ability to actively flex the right digits, but difficulty extending the index and middle fingers. Ashworth scale testing of the right upper limb yielded the following 
results: shoulder adductors and internal rotators: 1, elbow flexors: 1, elbow extensors: 0, wrist flexors and extensors: 0, finger and thumb flexors: 0.
The clinician suggested injecting botulinum toxin injection to the right elbow flexors and the right index and middle finger flexors. The patient asked the clini-
cian why the shoulder adductors will not be treated because she heard that the “score was a 1, while the fingers had a score of 0.” The clinician confirmed with 
the patient that she has no trouble cleaning her underarm or donning shirtsleeves, and he thus felt that muscle tightness alone is not a reason enough for treat-
ment. Intervention should be considered only if spasticity causes or predisposes to the development of problems and complications. Furthermore, the clinician 
pointed out that the Ashworth score, which is done passively, is not as important as the patient’s report of functional problems with the two finger flexors that tend 
to tighten up in relation to a particular task. The patient also asked about other treatment options. The clinician replied that he did not think that a problem that is 
limited to two muscle groups in a single limb justify the systemic side effects of oral drugs. He also pointed out that another injection technique using phenol car-
ries more risks for adverse events than botulinum toxin. The clinician and patient mutually agreed on the following goals, treatment plan, and measure of success:

Management
Nonpharmacologic
Although spasticity is a neurologically based condition, its obvi -
ous manifestation is physical, and thus physical modalities are a 
mainstay in the first line of treatment because they are widely 
available and innocuous relative to drugs. Passive stretching has 
been shown to be effective in reducing tone and increasing range 
of motion (ROM) in patients with spasticity after brain injury.243 
Splinting and casting are often used in the acute setting for sus -
tained stretching. 23,28,182,209,212 Casting alone seems sufficient to 
prevent contracture and reduce spasticity if the intervention is 
initiated early after severe brain injury. A systematic review on the 
use of upper extremity casting found high variability in casting 
protocols, which indicates no consensus in technique. 146 Indi -
vidualized stretching has been shown to be clinically promising to 
reduce wrist and finger spasticity and improve passive ROM. 51 
More recent studies have shown some effects on spasticity reduc -
tion after stroke. 142,252 The efficacy of electrical stimulation on 
spasticity reduction is not well established. 44 A recent review 36 
suggested that moderate-quality evidence exists for transcutane -
ous electrical nerve stimulation, neuromuscular electrical stimula-
tion, resistance training, and lower extremity ergometer training 
with or without functional electrical stimulation in decreasing 
poststroke spasticity. A novel technique demonstrated a long-
lasting effect on spasticity reduction if electrical stimulation was 
triggered by voluntary breathing. 156 Guided self-rehabilitation 
contracts, a diary-based rehabilitation strategy, wherein specific 
muscles are subjected to “high-load, home self-stretching tech -
niques,” is a promising recent addition to nonpharmacologic 
treatment strategies.98,99
Pharmacologic
There is more than one way to approach the pharmacologic man-
agement of spasticity. Historically, a stepladder paradigm was 
useful, beginning with the least invasive treatment and culminat -
ing with surgical procedures should the nonoperative options  
fail. More recently, this sequential approach has been abandoned 
in favor of concurrent use of both noninvasive and invasive  
procedures (e.g., injection therapy concurrent with therapeutic 
exercises, or using injection therapy for residual focal spasticity in 
persons whose generalized spasticity is simultaneously managed 
with intrathecal drugs). This reflects better appreciation of the mag-
nitude of the problem—that is, spasticity alone does not account 
for presenting problem, and other features of UMN syndrome 
contribute significantly. Consequently, different treatments may be 
required to increase the chances of successful outcome. There are 
also different methods when deciding on pharmacologic interven-
tion for spasticity. One approach is to base drug choice on the 
clinical presentation and the number of limbs involved. The algo-
rithm in Fig. 24.3 illustrates this.
Another method is to determine the choice of drug on the 
underlying pathophysiology of spasticity, which in turn is influ -
enced by neuroanatomic involvement in the disease process 33,60 
(Fig. 24.4). This can be particularly challenging because it is not 
always easy to determine the exact pathophysiologic process re -
sponsible for spasticity.
Oral Spasmolytics
Various medications with different mechanisms of action have been 
used to treat spasticity. The most commonly used are baclofen, a 
gamma-aminobutyric acid B (GABA-B) 57 agonist, tizanidine, an 
alpha2-adrenergic receptor agonist, and dantrolene, a hydantoin 
derivative (Table 24.4). Others include benzodiazepines, which are 
GABA-A agonists, and gabapentin, a likely selective inhibitor of 
voltage-gated calcium channels. Both are antiepileptic drugs 
(AED). Based on suggestions made by a few case reports or series, 
AEDs, including levetiracetam, pregabalin, phenytoin, and carba-
mazepine, appear to exert spasmolytic effects.29,109 Although their 
mechanisms of action vary, in general oral spasmolytics are associ-
ated with similar and related adverse events, such as sedation, 
drowsiness, and weakness. These side effects limit the effective 
utilization of oral spasmolytics in general. However, when dosed 
appropriately, oral spasmolytics can adequately control spasms and 
pain and facilitate such things as transfers and bed mobility in 
persons with SCI. A retrospective analysis that used medical and 
pharmacy claims data from a large, national US health plan specu-
lated that poor medication adherence could be a function of the 
adverse effects associated with oral baclofen, tizanidine, or dan -
trolene, or of the perception of the limited efficacy of these drugs. 
Oral drug adherence did not vary across patient populations, in -
cluding stroke, SCI, TBI, cerebral palsy (CP), and MS.105
In general, the evidence for therapeutic efficacy for all spasmo-
lytic agents is supported by only a few placebo-controlled trials. 
Consequently, there is no evidence-based treatment algorithm for 
drug choice and dosing. Instead, medication choice is based on 
practical reasons, such as favoring or avoiding certain medica -
tions, or in some situations capitalizing on certain known adverse 
effects. For instance, a person with SCI and painful nocturnal 
spasms may be best managed by tizanidine, which has analgesic 
properties independent of its spasmolytic effect. Because one of its 
common side effects is sedation, tizanidine may also help promote 
sleep that is disturbed by the spasms. However, if the same patient 
has orthostatic hypotension or is taking clonidine, another alpha 
agonist, tizanidine, may not be the best choice because of its  
potential hypotensive effect.
Medication dosages vary among persons largely because of the 
tolerability of side effects. Deficits in alertness and attention in 
persons with cerebral disease are magnified by the sedating effects of 
oral spasmolytics. It is not uncommon to bypass oral drugs in this 
patient population and instead use focal injection therapy as first-
line treatment. Owing to the therapeutic effect of spasmolytics,

in persons with incomplete SCI or MS, who may lose the ability 
to ambulate should the weakness become pronounced. In this 
situation, a lower dose of the spasmolytic drug is preferred even 
though spasticity will not be controlled as well. Another option is 
to use a combination of different medications albeit at lower doses 
to circumvent a particular side effect typical of a drug.
As presented earlier in this chapter, there are various indepen -
dent or interacting pathophysiologic processes that are postulated 
to cause spasticity. Yet, in the absence of solid scientific evidence, 
medication choices are typically empiric or to a certain extent based 
on a drug’s side effect profile. This common practice may explain 
why a particular medication is not consistently effective both within 
and across patient populations. Corollary to this, the fact that dif-
ferent pathomechanisms are involved because of varying disease 
types and severity or anatomic involvement may explain why a 
single medication cannot be always effective for a given disease 
condition. This also imposes an important challenge in designing 
comparative efficacy and safety caused by the heterogeneity of the 
likely pathomechanisms underlying the spastic condition and the 
mechanisms of action of various spasmolytic drugs.
Baclofen is an analog of GABA, the most potent inhibitory 
neurotransmitter. It binds to GABA-B receptors that are wide -
spread in Ia sensory afferent neurons and alpha motor neurons. 
It is the most widely studied oral spasmolytic in various patient 
populations, including SCI, MS, CP , and acquired brain injuries 
(TBI, stroke, anoxia, or encephalopathy).
16,64,168,171,177,206 As 
with many spasticity medication trials, the studies involving ba -
clofen demonstrated reduction in hypertonia and spasms but did 
not investigate functional impact. Adverse effects of baclofen 
include drowsiness and weakness, which are shared by other oral 
spasmolytics. Abrupt discontinuation of baclofen may result in a 
withdrawal syndrome characterized by rebound spasticity, hallu -
cinations, and seizures.
Tizanidine is believed to exert its effects by inhibiting the fa -
cilitatory ceruleospinal tracts and the release of excitatory amino 
acid from spinal interneurons. As a result, presynaptic interneuro-
nal excitation is suppressed, manifesting as reduction in tonic and 
phasic stretch reflexes and agonist-antagonist cocontraction.
244 
Similar to baclofen, its efficacy in reducing muscle hypertonia and 
clonus, but not in improving function, has been demonstrated by 
various investigations.
169 However, in patients with stroke or TBI, 
tizanidine was deemed inferior to botulinum toxin in efficacy and 
safety in treating upper limb spasticity.
241 In addition to the typi-
cal side effects of oral spasmolytics, hepatotoxicity may also occur. 
Thus monitoring the liver function test is important, especially in 
those patients who concomitantly take hepatically cleared drugs. 
Being a central alpha
2-adrenergic receptor agonist, tizanidine 
should be used carefully in patients with hypotension or are con -
comitantly taking other alpha agonists, such as clonidine. Like -
wise, caution should be used when coadministering tizanidine 
with fluoroquinolone antibiotics, which may increase the former’s 
serum concentration. Tizanidine is a peculiar spasmolytic in that 
it has a dose-dependent antinociceptive effect presumably caused 
by a reduction in the release of substance P and activity of excit -
atory amino acids at the spinal level.
222 This makes tizanidine a 
suitable choice for those with concurrent spasticity and pain.

Dantrolene is used to manage a myriad of conditions, such as 
neuroleptic malignant syndrome and 3,4-methylenedioxymeth -
amphetamine (Ecstasy) overdose, but its first reported therapeutic 
use was for spasticity.133 Unlike baclofen and tizanidine, which act 
on the central nervous system, dantrolene works directly on skel -
etal muscle. Although it is widely regarded to exert its muscle  
relaxant effect by inhibiting the release of calcium from the sarco-
plasmic reticulum during excitation-contraction coupling, its 
exact mechanism of action had not been elucidated until recently. 
It appears that a direct or indirect inhibition of ryanodine recep -
tor, the major calcium release channel of the skeletal muscle sar -
coplasmic reticulum, is fundamental in the molecular action of 
dantrolene in decreasing intracellular calcium concentration. 139 
Several investigations have supported dantrolene’s beneficial effect 
on spasticity.16,130,133,257 Although it is peripherally acting, dan -
trolene has also been associated with side effects that appear to be 
centrally mediated, such as drowsiness, dizziness, fatigue, and 
weakness, perhaps through alteration of neuronal calcium homeo-
stasis.75,130 Because of its potential for hepatotoxicity, regular 
monitoring of liver function is recommended.
A systematic review of 101 randomized trials46 found that ba -
clofen, tizanidine, and dantrolene are effective in reducing spasticity 
relative to placebo, particularly in the MS population. It also found 
evidence that baclofen and tizanidine are equivalent in effectiveness, 
but there was insufficient evidence to compare with dantrolene. The 
review also found fair evidence that the overall side effect rate be-
tween baclofen and tizanidine is similar and that the former is more 
associated with weakness and the latter with dry mouth.
Other agents that have been observed to exert a spasmolytic 
effect in various patient populations with UMN diseases include 
the alpha-adrenergic agonist clonidine, which has more affinity to 
alpha2 than alpha 1 receptors 160; 4-aminopyridine, a potassium 
channel blocker that is thought to enhance neural transmission by 
improving axonal conduction and synaptic neurotransmitter re -
lease, has also been shown to reduce spasticity in patients with 
SCI.211 Cyproheptadine, a serotonergic antagonist, has been dem-
onstrated to decrease spasticity in persons with SCI. 253 Cannabis 
and its active ingredient, delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), 
and cannabinoids have been shown to decrease spasticity, 52,218 
especially in those with pain. 106 The best evidence of efficacy for 
spasticity is in the use of a THC and cannabidiol oromucosal 
spray (Sativex) in MS. 197 Its use is limited owing to concerns 
about potential cognitive impairment and possible increased risk 
of psychosis associated with cannabis.
Focal Treatment—Botulinum Toxins
Chemodenervation using botulinum toxins has become a widely 
used spasticity treatment. It is recommended for the management 
of focal spasticity242 or when the treatment plan targets a particular 
muscle or group of muscles. Botulinum toxin exerts its effect 
through inhibition of acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular 
junction. The clinical effects of the toxin do not manifest until 
several days after an injection, largely because of a complex process 
involved, which includes (1) internalization of the toxin,  
(2) reduction and translocation of disulfide bonds holding the 
toxin’s light and heavy polypeptide chains, and (3) inhibition of 
acetylcholine release. The last one occurs because the internalized 
toxin cleaves so-called SNARE (soluble n-ethylmaleimide sensitive 
fusion attachment receptor) proteins that facilitate neuronal mem-
brane adherence of synaptic vesicles, a necessary step before extru-
sion into the neuromuscular junction. The clinical effects last about 
3 months, which was previously thought to be caused by neuronal 
resprouting. However, it has been shown that functional repair of 
the previously affected neuromuscular junctions is the likely 
mechanism for the duration of action.59 Until recently, there had 
been only seven serotypes (A, B, C1, D, E, F , G) of Clostridium 
botulinum identified. A newer serotype, botulinum toxin X, was 
then identified and characterized,273 and the following year a botu-
linum neurotoxin-like toxin discovered in a commensal strain of 
Enterococcus faecium was reported.272 At the current time only sero-
types A and B are utilized clinically and have US Food and Drug 
Administration (FDA)–approved indication for spasticity: abobot-
ulinumtoxinA, incobotulinumtoxinA, onabotulinumtoxinA, and 
rimabotulinumtoxinB. Recently, daxibotulinumtoxinA was ap -
proved by the FDA for cervical dystonia but not yet for spasticity. 
There are newer toxins, such as prabotulinumtoxinA, letibotu -
linumtoxinA, Meditoxin, Neuronox, and A2NTX, 47,86,92,225,245 
which will not be discussed here because they are primarily used for 
cosmetic indications or are not widely in use. All serotypes (except 
for the newly described toxin in an Enterococcus species) share com-
mon properties in that they are all clostridial neurotoxins with a 
bichain structure. They all inhibit acetylcholine release, and the 
muscle paralysis they produce is reversible. However, they are anti-
genically distinct, and they have varying neuronal binding sites and 
enzymatic actions on different SNARE proteins. They may also 
have different specificity. Nevertheless, the effects of all botulinum 
toxin A (BoNT-A) products are mediated by the 150 kDa BoNT-A 
neurotoxin. It was found that, at current FDA-approved doses, 
abobotulinumtoxinA contains greater amounts of active neurotoxin 
as compared with other BoNT-A products.72 Therefore it is impor-
tant to highlight that different botulinum toxin products are not 
interchangeable and cannot be converted into units of other prod-
ucts.71 Table 24.5 summarizes these differences and other properties 
of pharmaceutic preparations of these toxins.
Consensus and review papers support the use of botulinum 
toxins for the management of spastic conditions in pediatric and 
adult populations.58,220,238,240,254 Although overwhelming evidence 
demonstrates that botulinum toxin chemodenervation results in 
significant improvement at the body function and structure  
level,10,12,19,39,144,220,221,235,238,240,246,265 it has not been shown un -
equivocally to be significantly efficacious in improving the activity 
and participation domains of the International Classification of 
Functioning, Disability and Health. 6,31,41,76 Perhaps published 
studies failed to show impact on function caused by methodologic 
limitations, such as in subject selection and sensitivity of outcome 
measures.
Clinical issues related to botulinum toxin chemodenervation
Dosing. Clinical experience, regulatory and insurance coverage 
restrictions, and manufacturers’ recommendations based on a few 
studies largely dictate choice of doses of the various botulinum 
toxins. There are a handful of dose-ranging studies that define dose-
related therapeutic and adverse effects in spasticity. 9,20,45,97,119,239 
Dosages that are used in current practice, recommended by  
consensus statements265 and described in retrospective and obser-
vational studies, 62,67,82 appear to be higher than doses used in 
published randomized controlled studies. Other than these guide-
lines and recommendations for dosing based on severity of spas -
ticity and muscle size, there is no objective method of predicting 
response to a particular dose of botulinum toxin. In the future, 
techniques such as electromyography, ultrasound elastography, 
and quantitative modeling may assist in the assessment of spastic-
ity’s impact on muscle architecture and function and (eventually) 
with treatment decision making.42,88,201,204

The use of escalating doses of botulinum toxins was becoming 
common practice until safety concerns were raised and fueled by 
mandates from the FDA. Responding to reports suggestive of 
systemic spread of botulinum toxins, in 2009 the FDA required 
new label warnings and a risk mitigation strategy that requires 
clinicians to discuss the risks and provide written material that 
details the warnings. The current experience of many clinicians is 
that using dosages of inco- and onabotulinumtoxinA as high as 
600 to 800 units (U) is effective and safe.

et al.263 reported on the safety and efficacy of escalating doses of 
incobotulinumtoxinA up to 800 U. The REPAS scale improved 
significantly. The proportion of subjects achieving at least three of

the toxin. No neutralizing antibody was detected.
Dilution. It is believed that increasing the volume of botuli -
num toxin solution injected magnifies its therapeutic effects by 
facilitating the toxin’s ability to reach more motor end plates. This 
has been demonstrated in animal studies,
136,234 where muscle pa-
ralysis and atrophy were greater when a more dilute preparation 
(i.e., higher volume relative to dose, or lower concentration) of 
botulinum toxin is injected. Humans studies are equivocal in 
demonstrating superiority of higher volumes of botulinum toxin 
injections
81,150 largely because of methodologic limitations of 
studies, although some investigations have found that high- 
volume or end plate–targeted botulinum toxin injections result  
in more profound neuromuscular blockade and spasticity and 
cocontraction reduction, as compared with low-volume, non-end 
plate–targeted injections.
100 As much as high-volume injections 
appear attractive, it may be a double-edged sword in that it may 
facilitate distant spread of the toxin. Cases have been reported 
wherein patients with poststroke spasticity who receive large dilu-
tion volumes in proximal upper limb muscles developed transient 
weakness in the noninjected contralateral upper limb. Based on 
electrophysiologic abnormalities documented after the injection, 
weakness was attributed to neuromuscular blockade.
T echniques to enhance toxin effectiveness.  There is a lot of 
interest in techniques to enhance the clinical effects of botulinum 
toxin without concomitantly increasing the risk of adverse events. 
Prohibited by recommended total toxin dose and cost, clinicians 
continuously seek ways to extend the clinical effects of toxin 
through modification of injection technique and use of adjunctive 
modalities. Although no standard currently exists, spreading the 
toxin within a muscle by using a higher volume/more dilute toxin 
solution (see earlier) and injecting at multiple sites within a 
muscle are regarded as ways to increase effectiveness by spreading 
the toxin to reach more muscle end plates, where neuromuscular 
junctions lie. It has also been suggested that targeting the muscle 
innervation zone, not just the motor points, can optimize the ef -
fects of botulinum toxin administration.
104 In some muscles these 
areas where intramuscular nerve endings are distributed may also 
result in better outcomes.
121 Other techniques used to attempt 
enhancement of toxin effectiveness include guided injection by 
listening to electromyography (EMG) activity, identifying motor 
points through electrical stimulation, or visualizing target sites by 
ultrasonography. The superiority of one guidance technique over

may affect treatment decision making.
108 Studies are in progress to 
establish a relationship between echointensity and spasticity.
Adjunctive therapies. Adjunctive therapies are usually viewed 
as add-on treatment after a more important and impactful modal-
ity (e.g., physiotherapy after botulinum toxin injection). As an 
alternative approach, multimodal therapy
217 involves the concur-
rent or sequential use of one or more therapeutic modalities to a 
current intervention(s) to enhance the overall therapeutic out-
come of a concerted spasticity management effort. This is in 
contradistinction to the concept of adjunctive therapy, which es -
pouses the subsequent addition of one modality to magnify the 
effect of what is considered the primary treatment.
There is high-level evidence to suggest that adjunct therapies 
may improve outcomes after botulinum toxin injection.
180 The 
beneficial role of various adjunctive therapy modalities in enhanc-
ing clinical outcomes of botulinum toxin chemodenervation has 
been reported. Casting can enhance the effect of onabotulinum -
toxinA
69 because prolonged stretching of spastic muscles after 
botulinum toxin chemodenervation affords long-lasting therapeu-
tic benefit. Extracorporeal shock wave therapy (ESWT) has been 
shown to have a greater magnitude of enhancement of botulinum 
toxin enhancement than electrical stimulation, most likely thor -
ough modulation of muscle rheology and neurotransmis -
sion.
117,203,229 The role of adhesive taping is controversial.127,226 A 
commonly used technique to magnify the clinical effect of botu-
linum toxin chemodenervation is pairing it with superficial elec -
trical stimulation, which influences activity of synaptobrevin-2 
receptors that facilitate neuronal binding and subsequent uptake 
of botulinum toxins.
15,115,166,258 However, the best stimulation 
protocol has not been established.203
Reasons for poor response to botulinum toxin chemodenervation. 
Botulinum toxin is widely regarded as an effective treatment of 
spasticity, but the response to treatment varies among persons. In 
most cases, spasticity relief is not sufficient, while in others even 
relatively small doses may result in weakness and other adverse 
events. Table 24.6 summarizes potential reasons for poor out-
comes of botulinum toxin chemodenervation.
Immunoresistance. In addition to unintended toxin diffusion 
to neighboring nerve endings and to distant areas caused by yet  
to be defined mechanisms,219,247 other undesirable effects of botu-
linum toxin therapy include sustained blockade of neural trans -
mission that results in prolonged effects similar to anatomic  
denervation, such as muscle atrophy, and immunoresistance. The 
latter accounts for loss of responsiveness to the spasmolytic effects 
of botulinum toxins. Bioassay of neutralizing antibodies (NAB) to 
botulinum toxin is considered the gold standard in confirming 
immunoresistance, but more practical clinical tests, such as the 
frontalis antibody test or unilateral brow injection, can be easily 
performed by injecting a small dose of botulinum toxin in either 
the frontalis or corrugator muscle.
32 Another method to assess 
nonresponse is the extensor digitorum brevis (EDB) test that in -
volves measurement of compound muscle action potential ampli-
tude elicited by electrical nerve stimulation of the peroneal nerve 
before and after injection of a small dose of botulinum toxin into 
the EDB.
132
Based on early reports in the cervical dystonia population, 
high doses and frequent injections of botulinum toxin were iden-
tified as risk factors for immunoresistance. 101,275 This also pro -
vided support for the practice of allowing no less than 90 days in 
between exposures to botulinum toxins. A much higher incidence 
of antibody formation has been associated with cervical dystonia 
than in spasticity.
186 There is growing interest in incobotulinum -
toxinA, which is free of excipient proteins and, as such, may have 
a lower propensity to induce an immunogenic response relative to 
the other botulinum toxin preparations with complexing pro-
teins.
17,126 In systematic review that included 1234 participants 
(91% with stroke) from 14 studies published between 2002 and 
2018, NAB prevalence was about 2.6% with no difference among 
different toxins.
164 However, there are heterogeneous reports. In 
children with CP , high NAB frequencies of up to 30% have been 
described.
112 The most significant risk factors for antibody forma-
tion were frequent treatment and high dose per treatment and a 
short interval between injections. In a more recent cohort of pa -
tients with different neurologic impairments, 83 of 596 patients 
(13.9%) had measurable NAB.
1 The probability of developing 
antibodies increased with repeated treatment and was influenced 
by the BoNT-A formulation. The NAB rates were similar for 
aboBoNT-A and onaBoNT-A (6% and 7%, respectively), while no 
NABs were observed in patients treated exclusively with inco -
BoNT-A. Disease entity and treatment duration had no additional 
influence.
1 It is thus recommended to avoid booster injections or 
short-interval injections to reduce the risk of NAB induction.
It is of paramount importance to note that presence of NAB 
does not equate to nonresponse to treatment. 164 In this review, 
among the 32 NAB-positive participants, 23 (72%) were nonre -
sponders, but the treatment still seemed effective in 9 (28%). 
Among the 41 nonresponders, 23 (56%) were NAB positive and 
18 (44%) were NAB negative. In other words, the identification 
of nonresponse by NAB positivity had sensitivity of 56% and 
specificity of 99.6%. This complex relationship between antibody 
titers and clinical outcomes of BoNT-A injection is true in other 
patient populations, such as dystonia.
111
Repeated injections. In clinical practice, many patients receive 
multiple injections over a period of many years, sometimes de -
cades, and the long-term effects are not systematically docu -
mented. The fact that patients continue to receive botulinum 
toxin over a long period of time implies that the patients continue 
to benefit from the treatment without experiencing adverse 
events. Most studies involving the use of botulinum toxin for 
spasticity involve only a few cycles of injection. A rare few have 
reported safety and sustained efficacy up to five injection cycles

over a few years. 66,93,143 Santamato et al. 228 reported safety and 
efficacy of repeated high-dose incobotulinumtoxinA (up to 840 
U/session over eight sets of injections) over a 2-year follow-up 
period. In a real-world registry study, 68 high adherence (at least 
three sessions) to repeated onabotulinumtoxinA injections was 
observed over a 2-year period in a subset of patients.
Although these few studies claimed that repeated injections 
were safe, concern remains about the long-term effect of botuli -
num toxins on muscles. An animal study concluded that the 
contractile properties of target and nontarget muscles did not 
fully recover within 6 months of botulinum toxin injections treat-
ment protocol.79 The same investigators also found that after re -
peated botulinum toxin injections, muscle atrophy sets in and 
contractile material is replaced by fat.80 Recognition of botulinum 
toxin’s effects on muscle length and force248 is also emerging, al -
though how this translates clinically is still unclear. These con -
cerning findings need to be investigated further in clinical studies 
emphasizing muscle changes in recovery after botulinum toxin 
chemodenervation.
Another area that warrants further investigation is the determi-
nation of when other treatment interventions should be considered 
after repeated botulinum toxin injections. For example, how many 
botulinum toxin injections need to be done to address a spastic-
dystonic “clenched fist” before surgical release of the finger and 
thumb flexor tendons should be entertained? How many times 
should a person with spastic paraplegia receive botulinum toxins 
before intrathecal baclofen therapy is considered? The economic 
impact of these clinical decisions will also need to be weighed to 
better appreciate the cost effectiveness of spasticity interventions.
Duration of action. The duration of effect of BoNT-A is widely 
thought to be approximately 3 months, although a pharmacody -
namic model suggests that a single injection may extend up to  
6 months.191 Clinically observed duration of BoNT-A effect varies 
between patients, depending on various factors, such as dose used 
and clinical condition of the patient.18 The recently FDA-approved 
daxibotulinumtoxinA or cervical dystonia appears to have a longer 
duration of effect 50; a similar trend was observed in upper limb 
spasticity in a phase 2 investigation.196 Clearly, this is one aspect of 
botulinum toxin therapy that needs further elucidation.
Early treatment. How soon after spasticity onset can botuli -
num toxins be safely and effectively administered is not well 
established. A few studies reported that treatment as early as 3 to 
6 months of disease onset effectively manages muscle hypertonia 
and decreases risk of later complications, such as contracture 
development.73,114 A recent study showed that botulinum toxin 
injection at the emergence of spasticity, as early as 18 days post -
stroke, can effectively prevent development of joint contracture. 
At the same time, muscle weakness related to botulinum toxin did 
not affect the course of motor recovery. 159 Results of an explor -
atory, double-blind, randomized placebo-controlled trial221 using 
abobotulinumtoxinA 500 U in subjects with upper limb spasticity 
within 2 to 12 weeks poststroke suggested that early treatment 
significantly delayed time to reach reinjection criteria when com -
pared with placebo. In a retrospective analysis of clinical practice 
of botulinum toxin treatment, higher doses of toxins were used in 
the early-start group (on average, 6.4 weeks from stroke onset) 
compared with the late-start group (49.6 weeks from stroke onset); 
however, much longer intervals were needed for the subsequent 
injections (23.1 weeks in the early-start group vs. 14.6 weeks in the 
late-start group).267
Recovery of function. The effect of botulinum toxin is primar-
ily in decreasing muscle hypertonia caused by spasticity and other 
related conditions, such as rigidity. However, there are unusual, 
but not rare, cases when the outcome of botulinum toxin injec -
tion surpasses this expectation and results in increase in functional 
abilities, particularly when appropriate therapeutic exercise is in -
cluded in the treatment regimen. Although an obvious explana -
tion for the outcome in this particular case was the uncovering of 
latent finger and thumb extensor strength once flexor spasticity is 
controlled, alternative mechanisms should be entertained. Could 
it be that botulinum toxin merely controlled spasticity of the 
finger flexors, and it was the ensuing “forced use” and task-specific 
exercises that accounted for the increased function? Natural recov-
ery may be dismissed because the improvement occurred almost 
4 years after the onset of neurologic disease, but it may be postu -
lated that controlling spasticity may have allowed the progression 
of natural recovery that was stalled when spasticity set in. Did 
altering muscle hypertonia and stretch properties also modify af -
ferent input into the spinal cord and subsequently influenced 
motor output?181 Or could it be that botulinum toxins have yet 
to be defined direct central nervous system effects, as suggested by 
some reports?7,56,90
Therapeutic weakness. Botulinum toxin injection to spastic 
muscles concurrently results in both spasticity reduction and 
muscle weakness, the latter either as a latent phenomenon uncov-
ered by tone reduction or a direct consequence of the toxin. We 
have observed that, in some patients, when this weakness is ad -
dressed by task-specific exercises, it could result in improvement 
of voluntary control of the weakened muscles. For this reason, we 
coined it “therapeutic weakness.” This phenomenon of therapeu-
tic weakening is revealed in a recent case of improved voluntary 
grip control after BoNT-A injection.43
This concept is further supported by another study149 in which 
15 stroke survivors with finger flexor spasticity and residual finger 
extension received botulinum toxin injections to the finger  
flexors, and electrical stimulation to the finger extensors after 
injections for 4 weeks (5 days/week). Finger flexor spasticity was 
significantly reduced, and finger extensor strength and active 
hand function were significantly improved as well at 2 and 
6  weeks postinjections. It is well documented that botulinum 
toxin injections effectively reduce spasticity, pain, and abnormal 
positioning; however, botulinum toxin injections are unlikely to 
improve active upper extremity function, such as reaching and 
grasping.6,236 The finding that therapeutic weakness of spastic 
flexor muscles is associated with functional improvement is im -
portant in that it can reshape the goals of using this therapy. In 
particular, therapeutic weakness using botulinum toxin could be 
considered in stroke survivors who have residual voluntary exten-
sion in the upper extremity. Improvement in voluntary control of 
extensor muscles likely resulted from reduced reciprocal inhibi -
tion from the spastic flexors after injection. Previous results have 
shown that injections can paralyze afferent fibers 74 and block 
acetylcholine release presynaptically at neuromuscular junctions.
Focal Pharmacologic Treatment—Nerve Block (Phenol 
Neurolysis and Cryoneurolysis)
Phenol neurolysis. Before the introduction of botulinum tox -
ins, nerve block using either alcohol or phenol was the only op -
tion for focal spasticity management. It was used in the 1950s to 
chemically ablate nerves to manage cancer-related pain, and it 
was subsequently applied to relieve spasticity. Over time, percu -
taneous nerve blocks using phenol or alcohol proved to be effec -
tive in controlling focal spasticity across different populations, 
including CP , TBI, and stroke. 48,134 Phenol 5% to 7% and

CASE 24.3 
Delayed Recovery of Function
PJ is a 69-year-old, right-handed male who presented with a 3-year history of multiple cerebral insults. He was involved in a motor vehicle crash that resulted in a 
moderate TBI. During the difficult extrication from the vehicle, he lost blood that resulted in hypotension on the scene. While recovering from these in the intensive 
care unit, he had a brainstem stroke and a suspected hypoxic event. Upon recovery, he had proximal muscle weakness and profound difficulty with walking. He 
subsequently developed spasticity in the right upper limb, characterized by a clenched fist, involving severe flexion of the fingers and thumb, which prevented 
his caregivers from cleaning his hand and trimming his fingernails (Fig. 24.5A). He was on oral baclofen 10 mg three times daily at the time of presentation. On 
examination, he had limb weakness (at best 4/5 in most limbs) and Ashworth score of 4 in the right finger, metacarpal (MCP) and thumb flexors, and 1 in the wrist 
and elbow flexors. The third distal interphalangeal joint was hyperextended. Given the focality of the spasticity, the clinician suggested weaning the oral baclofen 
with intent to discontinue after about 4 weeks and botulinum toxin injection of the flexor digitorum superficialis (onabotulinumtoxinA 100 U) and profundus  
(75 U) and flexor pollicis longus (25 U). At around 1 month after injection, the Ashworth score of the finger and thumb flexors decreased to 2. The patient received 
another injection of onabotulinumtoxinA 3 months after the first injection to the same muscles using the same doses. An additional 20 U were injected into the 
lumbricals to address the MCP flexion spasticity. About 1 month later, the patient reported not only a significant decrease in tightness but also a new ability to 
actively extend his fingers and open his hand (see Fig. 24.5B–D). He also received occupational therapy that focused on forced-use activities and functional 
retraining. Subsequently, he recovered full active movement of the right hand and the ability to perform functional tasks, such as picking up objects, including 
utensils and a drinking glass during meals, combing his hair, and buttoning his shirt.

Therapeutic Weakness
TP is a 53-year-old, right-handed female who sustained a hemorrhagic right middle cerebral artery stroke 3 years earlier. She had finger flexor spasticity and 
residual weak finger/wrist extension. She received 50 U BoNT-A injection in both the left flexor digitorum superficialis and the flexor digitorum profundus. As

various activities of daily living. This correlated with reduced finger extensor muscle EMG activity (Fig. 24.6). The improvement in voluntary control of hand opening 
and grip release was likely realized by a decrease in the abnormal cocontraction of finger flexors during voluntary finger extension. This case suggested that a

Cryoneurolysis. Cryoneurolysis has decades of established ef -
ficacy in the analgesic literature. 120 Cryoneurolysis applied to 
motor nerves or motor branches is an innovative intervention for 
spasticity management. The cryoneurolysis procedure begins with 
identifying a target nerve under ultrasound guidance. Similar to 
phenol neurolysis, a cryoprobe is inserted percutaneously through 
a recommended angiocatheter after a weal of lidocaine is placed 
subcutaneously. The cryoprobe placement is confirmed when 
electrical stimulation at low intensity (,1 mA) is able to stimulate 
the target nerve and not the surrounding muscle. Cryoneurolysis 
is realized through the Joule-Thomson effect
120 (i.e., throttling a 
gas through an orifice from a high- to a low-pressure area within 
the cryoprobe), leading to a drop in temperature at the cryoprobe 
tip. The temperature is determined by the boiling point of the 
cryogen, carbon dioxide, or nitrous oxide, typically between 260 
and 288°C for pain or spasticity.
120 At this temperature, an ice 
ball with a diameter between 3.5 and 18 mm is formed in seconds 
along the distal cryoprobe by freezing the surrounding fluid. No 
fluid passes from the probe into the body. The ice ball causes a 
local zone of axon and myelin disruption leading to a secondary

extending bidirectionally outward from the lesion. A distinct fea-
ture is that cryoneurolysis does not cause damage to the basal 
lamina, epineurium, and perineurium of the targeted nerve, which 
serves as conduit for neural regeneration. The axon will regrow 
after 3 to 6 months, depending on the size of the nerve. 116 With 
cryoneurolysis, there is no dispersion of fluid away from the ice 
ball toward unwanted targets because the ice ball freezes in place 
and melts after a treatment cycle is completed. A cycle can last 
from seconds to minutes; a 106-second cycle uses a handhold Io -
vera device,259 and a 60-second cycle produces analgesia and hypo-
esthesia in animals. 185 Blood vessels are typically not affected by 
the cryoneurolysis at the temperatures utilized. They serve as a heat 
sink and are not thrombosed.87 Winston et al. have demonstrated 
feasibility and effectiveness of cryoneurolysis in spasticity manage-
ment through case reports and case series in recent years.161,260-262
Side effects of cryoneurolysis include localized skin effects, 
paresthesias, and numbness. Most are considered mild and lim -
ited to a few weeks to months. 199,214 Cryoneurolysis causes 
immediate damage to the nerve. Patients may experience burning 
and cramping and a painful dysesthesia during the procedure. In 
clinical experience, cryoneurolysis is more painful than other 
spasticity treatments, but it ends quickly because the nerve is  
fully lysed. The first analysis of side effects on 277 nerves in  
113 patients found 96.75% of nerves had no side effects after the 
treatment.259 Among 113 patients, there were three skin events 
that healed. Nine patients reported nerve pain or dysesthesia (in 
3.25% of the 277 nerves; two motor nerves and seven mixed 
motor sensory targets) with four patients requiring a medical 
treatment for this. All patients reported resolution on follow-up. 
Three patients’ symptoms remained at 3 months but resolved by 
6 months. No patients withdrew from the study because of side 
effects. There is no maximal dose because no drugs are adminis -
tered. Further clinical trials on its efficacy are needed.
Nerve blocks using phenol and alcohol have taken a backseat 
to chemodenervation with botulinum toxins, which appear to 
have a better safety profile and are easier to administer. Despite its 
favorable safety and efficacy profile, botulinum toxin treatment 
alone is often inadequate to fully control symptoms. Typical in -
surance coverage and FDA approval for spasticity limits the use  
of onabotulinumtoxinA to 400 to 600 U no more than every  
3 months. In those with moderate to severe spasticity, botulinum 
toxin treatment may not be effective even with a high dose of 
toxins. Nerve blocks with phenol and alcohol and cryoneurolysis 
can provide alternative treatment options or can be used in com -
bination with botulinum toxin treatment.

Intrathecal Therapies
As early as the 1950s, intrathecal phenol was used to reduce pain 
and spasticity. Its popularity has waned owing to its many potential 
complications, such as sensory, respiratory, bladder, bowel, and 
sexual dysfunction, and the introduction of baclofen, which ap -
pears to have a better adverse effect profile. The use of intrathecal 
phenol is now limited to specific situations where severe spasticity 
cannot be managed through any of the other currently available 
techniques.125 Other drugs have been administered intrathecally 
primarily to manage pain, among which are opioids, gabapentin, 
and clonidine; the last one has a possible spasmolytic effect.175 Con-
tinuous intrathecal baclofen (ITB) was introduced in the 1980s to 
treat spasticity in SCI. Since then, several trials demonstrated similar 
beneficial effects in other patient populations, such as CP ,2 brain 
injuries,173 stroke,172 and MS. 200 In addition, ITB was shown to 
benefit stroke survivors with severe spasticity by decreasing pain 
and improving quality of life.55 A recent series of articles from an 
expert panel has specifically advised on best practices for ITB ther-
apy, including patient selection, 231 screening test, 27 dosing and 
long-term management,26 and troubleshooting.230
The physiologic effect of ITB involves a decrease in mono- and 
polysynaptic reflex responses147 and reduction in resistance to pas-
sive stretch.232 At the cellular level, ITB’s mechanism of action is 
similar to that of the oral form; however, it has the advantage of 
more direct access to GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord be -
cause the blood-brain barrier will not have to be traversed. Thus 
intrathecal administration allows greater hypertonia reduction 
and reflex inhibition at doses lower than the oral form, thereby 
decreasing the risk of adverse events.
ITB is usually considered only when less invasive management 
options are ineffective in controlling spasticity, although utilization 
within the first few months of disease onset may be warranted in 
situations where spasticity of the lower limbs is so severe that wait-
ing for other less invasive options to take effect may predispose to 
more complications. An investigation on the use of ITB within  
3 months of acquired brain injuries showed significant reduction 
of spasticity and spasm frequency at 1-year follow-up, and their 
global outcome measures did not differ from those who received 
ITB therapy later (between 3 and 6 months of disease onset). 210 
Another situation where very early use has been shown to be ben-
eficial is in persons with severe dysautonomia after a brain injury.83 
Concern exists that early use of ITB may retard recovery because 
of the inhibitory effects of GABA,30 but a more recent study sug-
gests otherwise. In an animal model, ITB treatment was initiated 
at 1 week after closed TBI and maintained for 1 month. It ap -
peared to block the early onset of spasticity and significantly di -
minish late-onset spasticity and anxiety-like behavior, without 
causing any significant adverse effect on cognitive and balance 
performance of rats. Concurrently, it was observed that there was 
a marked upregulation of GABA, norepinephrine, and brain-  
derived neurotropic factor expression in spinal cord tissue.25
The intrathecal system consists of a programmable pump that 
has an accessible drug reservoir that is implanted subcutaneously 
in the abdominal area. A catheter that is connected to the im -
planted pump’s side port is tunneled under the skin and enters the 
spine at the lumbar level. The catheter tip is advanced cephalad 
and usually left at the thoracic level, although it is not uncommon 
for it to be placed at the cervical area3 or sometimes intraventricu-
larly.249 Before surgical implantation of the catheter and pump, a 
trial is performed to confirm ITB’s effectiveness in decreasing 
spasticity, spasms, or pain. The trial typically involves bolus ad -
ministration of baclofen into the intrathecal space through a  
spinal needle. An alternative method is to continuously infuse 
baclofen intrathecally through an external catheter.107 Given risks 
associated with this technique, performance of the trial usually 
requires hospital admission, as compared with the former method, 
which is carried out in the ambulatory setting. Superiority of one 
over the other has not been established, although the former ap -
pears to be more commonly employed because it is less labor or 
resource intensive. A successful screening trial is generally defined 
as an Ashworth scale reduction of 1 to 2 points; however, in cer -
tain situations, the Ashworth scale is not always applicable.  
For instance, the indication for considering ITB may be painful, 
intermittent spasms, where use of the Ashworth scale as an assess-
ment tool and outcome measure is not valid. Sometimes, the

clinicians, patients, and caregivers determine whether intrathecal

ommended intrathecal bolus dose of baclofen during screening 
trial is 50 mg. If ineffective, the option is to try higher doses

underlying weakness immediately after the ITB screening trial. 
First, the patient must be assured that weakness during the trial  
is reversible and that it does not mean that the same degree of 
weakness will occur after ITB pump implantation. The finding 
of increased postscreening trial weakness merely demonstrates 
that ITB has the potential to abolish spasticity and suggests that  
the patient will most likely require a low dose of ITB after pump 
implantation. Thus instead of starting with the “usual” starting 
dose after pump implantation (determined by doubling the dose 
used during the screening trial that resulted in significant decrease 
in spasticity), the starting daily dose may be the same bolus dose 
used during the trial. T o illustrate, if a patient experienced good 
control of spasticity or spasms with a 50-m
g bolus of baclofen but 
also experienced an increase in weakness, the initial dose after 
pump implantation may be 50 mg infused continuously over a 
24-hour period. Another strategy to assuage concerns about exces-
sive weakness during the screening trial is to use a lower dose (e.g., 
25-mg bolus). Although 97% of patients with spasticity resulting 
from SCI
198 and 94% with cerebral origin spasticity 91 respond 
positively to bolus screening trials, it is still advantageous to per-
form the screening trial to allow the patients and caregivers an 
opportunity to experience a state of low or no spasticity. The trial 
results can also guide the physician regarding future dose targets 
and a patient’s peculiar response to intrathecal baclofen.
When planning ITB therapy, nonmedical conditions need to 
be considered, such as the patient’s ability to comply with dose 
titration and pump management, which will require regular visits 
with the clinician. Management does not end with implantation 
of the pump; frequent dose adjustments will be needed subse -
quently to reach appropriate ITB dose. We define an optimal dose 
as one that adequately controls (not completely abolishes) spastic-
ity without causing any unwanted effect. Thus optimal dose can 
be determined only by titrating the dose upward until a side effect 
occurs (e.g., excessive weakness, bladder retention, constipation) 
and then decreasing to a level that does not cause the adverse 
event. The frequency of regular refills of the pump reservoir
