Surgical Intervention
Surgical management of spasticity is a well-accepted treatment 
option for contractures because it primarily addresses joint defor-
mities rather than spasticity itself. Surgical interventions include 
neuroablative procedures, such as peripheral neurotomies and 
dorsal rhizotomies, and orthopedic reconstructive procedures, 
such as tendon lengthening and tendon transfer. Surgical inter -
vention is a permanent treatment. Optimal management of spas -
ticity by nonsurgical means should be attempted before surgical 
treatment is employed. Therefore it is often viewed as a treatment 
of last resort. However, surgical intervention could be pursued 
earlier in cases where the underlying problem is best treated with 
surgery or when other options are unavailable because of lack of 
availability or resources.89 When excessive spasticity and contrac -
ture are not sufficiently controlled by therapy and pharmacologic 
treatment, tendon lengthening is often considered. This involves 
correction of abnormal joint posture alignment, allowing for im -
proved ability to move joints and in many cases facilitate activity 
and exercise.131 Common tendon procedures include split ante -
rior tibial tendon transfer and Achilles tendon lengthening to 
manage spastic equinovarus. 61 T endon lengthening and release 
can also help with upper limb management.5 T endon lengthening 
could be performed with minimal invasive technique via percuta-
neous needle tenotomy.53 Surgical interventions primarily manage 
joint deformities rather than spasticity itself. T endon lengthening 
elongates the tendon and subsequently corrects the abnormal 
joint position, but it does not change the contractile and me -
chanical properties of muscle and its innervation. In other words, 
the unchanged spastic muscle has a new resting position at a cor -
rected joint position via the elongated tendon. The reflex responses 
are both velocity and muscle (not tendon) length dependent 155 
and related to muscle fiber length and sarcomere numbers.77,85,157 
The exaggerated reflex responses are still expected to remain un -
changed if the same stretch is applied with reference to the new 
resting position. Therefore tendon lengthening can improve pos -
ture but is not likely to correct altered stretch reflex in spastic 
muscles. Other procedures, such as neurectomy, hyperselective 
neurectomy, neurotomy, and hyperselective neurotomy, have also 
been shown to benefit spastic conditions.8,37,148,176
Emerging Therapy—Hyaluronidase Injections
Advances in understanding pathogenesis of poststroke spasticity 
(see Fig. 24.1) not only allow a better understanding of the rela -
tion between spasticity and motor recovery but lead to the devel -
opment of new treatments as well. One example is the use of 
human recombinant hyaluronidase to address the active muscle 
stiffness component of spastic hypertonia.216 The accumulation of 
hyaluronan within spastic muscles is considered the primary ex -
tracellular mechanism promoting development of muscle stiff -
ness. It was then hypothesized that hyaluronidase, the enzyme of 
hyaluronan, is expected to reduce muscle stiffness if it is injected 
into the muscles. 216 In their pioneering study, Raghavan et al. 
injected human recombinant hyaluronidase with saline in 20 pa -
tients with unilateral upper limb spasticity at a single visit. The 
CASE 24.5 
LY is a 27-year-old female in a minimally conscious state secondary to 
anoxic brain damage 10 years earlier, who presented to the emergency 
room with generalized seizures. Before this, her seizures have been well 
controlled with carbamazepine. Her mother reported that a few days 
earlier the patient “just did not seem like herself” and that since the day 
before seemed “a lot tighter in the legs than usual.” The mother reported 
that the patient’s urine seemed to be cloudier, but did not pay much 
attention to this. The mother had difficulty pointing out the exact day she 
noticed these changes in the patient because of her daughter’s inability to 
meaningfully communicate. Past medical history is significant for seizure 
disorder that developed after the anoxic event but has been well controlled 
by carbamazepine. An ITB pump was implanted 7 years earlier to better 
control spasticity and extensor posturing. Current ITB dose is 974.8 mg/
day, which controls lower limb spasticity well enough to facilitate care and 
transfers, and the most recent intrathecal pump refill was 6 weeks earlier. 
Aside from carbamazepine and baclofen, the patient was not on any other 
medication. At the emergency room, she also had a temperature of 102.1°F. 
Blood pressure was 120/70 mm Hg, and heart rate was 110 beats/min. The 
mother added that the patient seemed more restless and uncomfortable. 
The patient was subsequently admitted to the critical care unit, where 
temperature increased to 104°F. Efforts to decrease hyperthermia using a 
cooling blanket and acetaminophen failed to resolve fever. While awaiting 
the blood and urine test results, including culture, she was empirically 
started on broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics because the clinician was 
suspecting sepsis that may have triggered the seizures. Chest radiograph 
was normal, and a noncontrast brain computerized tomography did not 
show any acute lesion. Six hours after admission to the unit, she had status 
epilepticus despite intravenous lorazepam and phenytoin. Temperature 
climbed to 106.8°F. Urinalysis, complete blood count, and electrolytes were 
normal. Later that evening, the patient went into cardiac arrest.
Intrathecal Baclofen Withdrawal
The accompanying case demonstrates the importance of recog-
nizing the signs and symptoms of ITB withdrawal, especially 
when they appear concurrently with other abnormalities. A suspi-
cion of sepsis-triggered seizure in this case was justified because of 
the report of changes in urine character and fever. However, there 
were other earlier signs that should have tipped about ITB with -
drawal, such as recurrence of spasticity (“…a lot tighter in the legs 
than usual…”). This, plus the nonspecific change in the overall 
condition (“…just did not seem like herself…”), sudden recur -
rence of seizures, and fever, should have raised the suspicion of 
ITB withdrawal in its advanced stage. The ITB pump should have 
been interrogated to assess function, a plain radiograph of the 
pump and catheter should have been done to assess gross catheter 
integrity, and intrathecal or enteral bolus of baclofen should have 
been considered. ITB withdrawal evolves over 1 to 3 days 49 and 
usually results from an abrupt decrease in GABA activity. Sup -
portive measures and drugs, such as benzodiazepines, intravenous 
dantrolene,135 and cyproheptadine, 174 may help mitigate with -
drawal symptoms, but the definitive treatment is restitution of 
ITB delivery. Fig. 24.7 presents an algorithm for troubleshooting 
suspected ITB problems.

lowed by somnolence, headache, convulsion, dizziness, and urinary

modalities are emerging as potential primary or adjunctive spas -

Summary
Spasticity is a commonly seen with many neurologic conditions. 
While the term “spasticity” chiefly refers to muscle tightness in 
response to stretch, it has become a catch-all word to describe the

clinical phenomenon of the upper motor neuron syndrome, includ-
ing other impairments that lead to hypertonia and dysfunction. 
Recent developments suggest that the clinical observations of “spas-
ticity” are not solely from central nervous pathology but also may be 
a consequence of concomitant and consequential intramuscular 
changes. Data acquisition and physical examination should go be-
yond spasticity assessment at rest, but take into consideration the 
consequences of spasticity on well-being and function. This, in turn, 
will inform the formulation of treatment goals, which should be 
personalized and mutually agreed upon by the clinician and patient. 
There are multiple pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic treat -
ment options. A multi-modal management approach is recom -
mended for best clinical outcome. The resurgence of the popularity 
of surgical treatment may reflect the recently-revived recognition of 
expanding treatment options beyond the algorithmic stepwise ap-
proach. Emerging treatments for spasticity include new medications 
(mucosal, injectable), cryoneurolysis, various physical modalities 
and neuromodulation interventions.
